question.text: What is the significance of the record date for IDCW (dividend) payment in mutual funds?

answer.text: Investors who hold units on the record date are eligible to receive the IDCW payment
It is the date when the IDCW amount is decided by SEBI
It is the last date to invest in the scheme
It is the date when the fund manager rebalances the portfolio

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The record date is the date set by the mutual fund to determine which investors are eligible to receive the IDCW payout. Only investors who hold units on the record date will receive the IDCW. Investors who purchase units after the record date will not receive the declared IDCW for that period. **The Trap:** The record date is not the same as the IDCW declaration date or the payment date. The fund declares the IDCW, sets a record date, and then pays on the payment date. Buying units just before the record date does not create any extra value since the NAV drops by the IDCW amount. **Cross-Exam Context:** Also tested in V-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Warn retired investors against buying units just before the record date thinking they will get "free" IDCW. The NAV drops by the IDCW amount on the ex-date, so there is no real gain. In fact, they may create unnecessary tax liability.
